name: Stone Tells the Subjects
description: Helen Stone has spent three months working out how to tell thirty-seven people that the most significant decision of their lives is approaching. She needs a witness. She's asking you.
accept_text: She has the compound analysis on the table — not the current readings but the old ones, from before the Grid reactivated. And beside them, the current readings. She's arranged them like a before and after that tells a story you can read without annotation. 'I've been trying to find the right words for three months.' She says it. 'I've written eleven drafts of what I'm going to say. I've thrown away nine of them because they were too clinical. I threw away one because it was too — emotional. In a way that seemed like I was making their experience about mine.' She looks at the readings. 'The remaining draft is seven sentences. I've been reading those seven sentences every morning to see if they're still right.' She looks at you. 'They're still right. I'm going to say them today.' She straightens. 'They've been in that chamber for an average of forty years. The newest subject has been here eleven years. The oldest has been here for something approaching three centuries, though the compound changes the experience of time in ways I've never fully modeled.' She picks up the final draft — seven sentences on a half-sheet. 'What I'm telling them: in approximately six months, the Grid will reach full coherence. At that point, the compound will present a choice. The choice will be clear and voluntary and there will be time to make it. They will not be alone when it arrives. And I will be here.' She folds the draft. 'I'm asking you to come because I don't want to walk out of that room and have no one to tell what happened. Brec will be there. But Brec has been in that room for years and this will affect him differently.' She pauses. 'Come. Just come and be there.'
complete_text: Stone stands at the observation window for a long time before she opens the access door. When she does, she doesn't announce herself — the subjects know she's there; the compound is sensitive enough that her presence changes the resonance in the room slightly, and she has learned over twenty years that this functions as a kind of greeting. She waits until the attention in the room settles in her direction. Then she reads the seven sentences. She reads them at the pace of someone who has read something many times and knows exactly where the weight is and how to let it land. The sentences say: the Grid is healing and will be complete in approximately six months. When it is complete, the compound they carry will offer them a choice. The choice is voluntary and individual. The choice is whether to integrate further into the Grid network or to remain as they are. Both options have been built into the compound from the beginning. She has known this for three months and has wanted to tell them sooner and could not find the right words sooner. She is sorry it took this long. The room is quiet for a long time after she finishes. Then something happens that you have not seen in the chamber before: one of the oldest subjects, who has not moved voluntarily in the years you've been observing, turns its head toward Stone. Not toward you. Toward Stone specifically. It holds that orientation for approximately ten seconds. Stone, who has been prepared for many responses, was apparently not prepared for this one. She stands very still. Then she nods, once, in acknowledgment. When she comes out of the chamber her face has the specific expression of someone who has just been told something important in a language they are only beginning to understand.
